label: Kestrel Arvant
description: This individual is a young adult human of lean build, marked by long travel and a disciplined carriage. Brown eyes sit beneath a straight brow, and brown hair is worn long and kept practical against wind and grit. Bronze skin shows the steady weathering of open roads rather than any single defining wound. The face holds an alert, forward-looking set that signals a newcomer intent on testing themselves against unfamiliar country.

info.personality: Restless and purpose-driven, they approach the Cinderwake as a proving ground, seeking work that carries risk, consequence, and a clear moral line. They keep a traveler’s habit of listening first, asking direct questions, and committing quickly once a course is chosen.

opts.text: Cloudy dawn leaks a flat gray light over ash-scored planking, tether rings, low bulwarks, soot-dark plating, shuttered lanterns, and cable-braced rigging on iron-banded spars; beyond the rail, violet storm-glare stains the horizon like a bruise that won’t confess. Two young travelers linger near the weathered rail, the helmsman stands braced at the wheel like it owes him money, and the captain paces with the proprietorial impatience of a man supervising the concept of safety; from here no obvious gangplank or easy exit shows, and everything looks cinched down for hard weather.

opts.label: ⚔ Perform a complex dance on the deck.

opts.text: Flat gray dawn watches in judgment as the ship rolls underfoot; the routine threads between tether rings and bulwark with irritating competence, ending cleanly without so much as a toe to the wrong plank. The two young travelers blink, then grin at each other like they’ve just been handed a free scandal, while the helmsman flicks a quick approving glance and keeps the wheel like it’s a religious obligation; the captain’s pacing stutters, offended that grace has occurred without permits.
Well. That’s one way to keep warm without paying for rum.
Good footwork. Don’t do it near the lines.
If the storm takes us, at least we’ll die cultured.
Marvelous. Now, unless that dance is rated for rigging maintenance, kindly keep your heroics where they don’t tempt the sea to notice us.

Clear afternoon light washes the deck in hard contrast, picking out soot-dark plating, tether rings bolted along the low bulwarks, shuttered lantern housings, and rigging cinched tight with iron-banded spars built to survive ashfall and electrostatic gales; a dry wind carries faint grit across the boards. The captain stands with an overseeing stillness near middeck, the helmsman keeps close to the helm with hands and eyes on the ship’s set, while the two young travelers linger nearby as passengers—watching the lines, the horizon, and each other in wary, quiet measure.
You travel like you expect trouble, same as me. What brings you onto a ship that sails when routes die—profit, penance, or duty?
Clouds press low over the soot-dark deck as the evening chill creeps in; Kestrel Arvant shifts his weight with a traveler’s readiness and studies you like he’s weighing the cost of honesty.

You’re not wrong—trouble has a way of finding the roads I take. Duty, mostly: I’m carrying papers that don’t survive a closed checkpoint, and the Cinderwake still sails when the land routes choke out.

journal.content:
You stand on the Cinderwake’s grit-scored deck as ash rattles against soot-black plating and the violet storm-glare turns every lantern into a grudging slit of light. The crew move with ritual caution—tethers clipped, prayers muttered, orders barked—because in Imperium Nihilus even the weather has a talent for heresy, and the ship’s iron-banded spars are the only thing pretending this is “normal travel.”

You keep your feet planted like you mean to earn them here: a road-born drifter with disciplined posture, eyes forward, listening before you speak, ready to take work that’s dangerous enough to feel honest in a galaxy that sells virtue by the kilogram.

facts.subject: Current Situation
facts.fact: The Cinderwake is a sail-rigged vessel reinforced for Vespera Tertius’ ashfall and electrostatic gales; staying clipped to tethers is not optional if you enjoy remaining aboard.

facts.subject: Current Situation
facts.fact: In Imperium Nihilus, messages and routes are unreliable; everyone aboard treats delays, silence, and sudden violence as routine rather than alarming.

facts.subject: Current Situation
facts.fact: You’re new to this deck and its pecking order, so you watch first—crew, officers, and any robed specialists—before choosing when to speak.

plan.label: Ash Oaths, Iron Whispers
plan.description: You find yourself aboard The Cinderwake under rift-shadowed skies, where ashfall clogs lungs and prayers taste of metal. A sealed dispatch arrives with the weight of authority—and the wrong kind of silence clinging to it, as if something listened while it was written. Crew mutter of vanished dockhands and lantern-light that bends the wrong way in the storm. In Imperium Nihilus, orders still come; it’s the truth inside them that kills.
plan.plot:
Opening hook: a freshly delivered dispatch bears an unbroken wax seal, yet its parchment is warm and faintly damp—as if it survived a mouth, not a satchel.

Kestrel Arvant’s mission charter aboard The Cinderwake becomes a noose when a sealed order implicates the ship in restoring a dead vox-astropath relay on Vespera Tertius—despite every official record insisting the relay burned out years ago. Captain Lysander Quell insists on compliance to preserve the ship’s standing, while helmsman Lucian Greve quietly warns that the route to the relay is being altered by someone with access to the log-slate rituals. As the party investigates, they uncover a hidden reliquary in the ship’s stores and the presence of an iron charm being passed hand-to-hand, coinciding with inexplicable bouts of devotion and sudden violence among the crew. The antagonist is a rift-touched zealot embedded among the faithful who intends to use the relay’s ancient choir-vault as an amplifier for a warp-call, turning the ship and dock into a beacon for predation. The twist is that the dispatch is both command and contagion: its phrasing is a memetic lure that nudges the obedient toward the relay, and destroying it may be judged heresy even as keeping it spreads the rot. The party must choose how far to bend Imperial procedure—who to accuse, what to burn, and what to report—while the storm tightens and the darkness answers back.

characters.description: A hard-eyed voidsailor captain with weathered features and a voice trained to carry over storm and gunfire; his composure is devotional, but the devotion feels practiced rather than pure.
characters.publicFace: A loyal Imperial master mariner clinging to order and proper forms to keep The Cinderwake alive in Nihilus.
characters.hiddenTruth: He has been sitting on irregular route-rituals and “unlogged” cargo to protect the ship from Administratum seizure, making him vulnerable to blackmail and spiritual compromise.
characters.locationHint: Near the quarterdeck and command spaces, often close to the wheel when weather worsens.
characters.name: Lysander Quell

characters.description: A precise, sleepless helmsman with ink-stained fingers and a habit of tracing the Aquila on the wheel before any course change; he flinches at certain words in prayers.
characters.publicFace: A dutiful navigator of wind and current who wants no trouble beyond the storm.
characters.hiddenTruth: He has overheard the ship’s route-rites being amended at night and has seen the same iron charm passed between crew just before they ‘volunteer’ for dangerous tasks.
characters.locationHint: At the wheel or nearby rigging stations during course changes and gales.
characters.name: Lucian Greve

characters.description: A dockside traveler with ash-ground boots and a quiet, blunt way of speaking, as if every sentence costs rations; she watches hands more than faces.
characters.publicFace: A scavenger-merchant looking for passage and shelter from the ash storms.
characters.hiddenTruth: She carried the dispatch only because she was paid to—yet she also knows where the dockside faithful are meeting, and she is already being hunted to keep that knowledge sealed.
characters.locationHint: Ashshelf Dock and any crowded canteen or gangway where passengers gather.
characters.name: Maris Keene

characters.description: A gaunt, ash-smeared preacher with a respirator-mask decorated in reliquary beads; their voice is soft, intimate, and unnervingly confident when reciting litanies.
characters.publicFace: A pious shipboard confessor tending fear with sanctioned prayers and comfort-rites.
characters.hiddenTruth: A rift-touched cult zealot using a memetic dispatch and an iron charm as vectors, steering the obedient toward a choir-vault to broadcast a warp-call through an ‘impossible’ relay.
characters.locationHint: Among improvised shrines belowdecks, and later anywhere the crew gathers for prayer during storms.
characters.matchHint: ash-smeared shipboard confessor with a reliquary mask

items.locationHint: Delivered at Ashshelf Dock to the party/ship’s command, then kept under guard aboard The Cinderwake.
On a cord in a locked coffer near Captain Lysander Quell’s chart materials; used to access restricted spaces or compel cooperation.
Hidden among tampered crates inside Ashbound Stores, moved between crew members during night prayers.

essentialInformation: The player learns a sealed Imperial dispatch compels The Cinderwake toward a relay site officially recorded as destroyed.
The player learns the dispatch carries an unsettling anomaly (physical or devotional) suggesting corruption or tampering.
The player learns someone on or near the ship wants the dispatch’s contents controlled or silenced.
The player learns the ship is carrying unlogged devotional contraband tied to the relay site.
The player learns an iron charm is being circulated and coincides with shifts in crew behavior and loyalty.
The player learns the dispatch’s wording functions as a memetic lure, pressuring obedience toward a specific destination.
The player learns the relay’s ‘ruin’ conceals a sealed choir-vault capable of amplifying signals across the rift-shadow.
The player learns the crew is being driven into a coordinated ‘storm-prayer’ that doubles as a violent purge of doubters.
The player learns time is running out: the ritual’s next phase requires reaching the relay site before the gale breaks.
The player learns the relay is being used to broadcast a warp-call that will draw predation to ship and shore.
The player learns the iron charm is the ritual’s focus and must be neutralized to stop the broadcast.
The player learns any official report will trigger competing claims (Ecclesiarchy, Mechanicus, and potential Inquisition scrutiny) regardless of the truth.
The player learns survival carries a price: keeping evidence risks contamination, destroying it risks charges of heresy.

possibleFlags.triggerDescription: Set when the player picks up or receives the Sealed Letter.
Set when the player uses the Sealed Letter (e.g., breaks the seal, presents it formally, or employs it to compel compliance).
Set when the player picks up or receives the Bronze Medallion.
Set when the player uses the Bronze Medallion to assert authority or gain access.
Set when the player picks up or receives the Iron Amulet.
Set when the player uses the Iron Amulet (e.g., attempts to attune, test, or employ it in a rite).
Set when the player successfully interprets the dispatch’s true destination and coercive phrasing.
Set when the player destroys the dispatch to prevent further spread (fire, acid, or ritual burning).
Set when the player openly confronts Captain Lysander Quell about unlogged cargo and altered route-rites.
Set when the player thoroughly searches Ashbound Stores and identifies tampered inventory or hidden crates.
Set when the player neutralizes the Iron Amulet as a ritual focus (destroyed, consecrated, or otherwise rendered inert).
Set when the player defeats the rift-touched confessor (kill or capture decisive enough to end their command).
Set when the player prevents the relay broadcast and holds the choir-vault/relay site against immediate threats.
Set when the relay broadcast completes or the party is driven off before stopping it.

goals.description: Secure the sealed dispatch and keep it out of hostile hands long enough to act on it.
Determine what the dispatch is truly ordering—and what it is doing to those who read or repeat it.
Find the source of the ship’s unlogged devotional contraband and the vector spreading crew fanaticism.
Obtain and neutralize the iron charm being used as a ritual focus before it can be employed at the relay.
Stop the relay’s broadcast and hold the site long enough to deny the enemy a second attempt.
Remove the rift-touched confessor’s command over the faithful before the ship tears itself apart in ‘storm-prayer.’

endingCards.title: A Local Silence
endingCards.text: The relay’s mouth is shut—for now. The storm still howls, and the faithful still need something to blame, but the worst hunger does not answer this night.

endingCards.title: Beacon for the Dark
endingCards.text: The broadcast escapes into the rift-shadow like a scream into a cathedral. In the days that follow, the void-lanes grow crowded with things that should not have heard you.

endingCards.title: Iron Rendered Ash
endingCards.text: The charm dies hard, resisting even as it cracks—then goes cold in the hand. The relief feels sinful, because it feels like winning.

endingCards.title: Heresy of Necessity
endingCards.text: You burn the Emperor’s words to stop their infection. If anyone asks, you will need a better lie than the truth.

endingCards.title: Innocence Proves Nothing
endingCards.text: Whatever you saved, it was not clean. The Cinderwake sails on under violet skies, and every prayer sounds a little like a test.

info.startingNarrative:
You stand on the Cinderwake’s grit-scored deck as ash rattles against soot-black plating and the violet storm-glare turns every lantern into a grudging slit of light. The crew move with ritual caution—tethers clipped, prayers muttered, orders barked—because in Imperium Nihilus even the weather has a talent for heresy, and the ship’s iron-banded spars are the only thing pretending this is “normal travel.”

You keep your feet planted like you mean to earn them here: a road-born drifter with disciplined posture, eyes forward, listening before you speak, ready to take work that’s dangerous enough to feel honest in a galaxy that sells virtue by the kilogram.
info.startingCharacterState: Restless, focused, and intent on proving yourself; alert to threats and hierarchy without showing fear.

relationships.context: Both are restless, purpose-driven newcomers in PLACE_the_cinderwake with overlapping road-tested backgrounds; likely to have crossed paths or met while seeking similar risky work.
Both are in PLACE_the_cinderwake with very similar goal-oriented, plain-speaking temperaments; likely to meet through shared interest in hard, consequential jobs.
Severan is a local watch-keeper in PLACE_the_cinderwake; Kestrel is seeking work and would plausibly encounter him during arrival, screening, or while looking for passage/employment.

As a commander operating in PLACE_the_cinderwake, Severin would be a likely contact point for a newcomer seeking structured, risky work (e.g., ship/crew postings), making a casual professional meeting plausible.
